content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
1102 Q Street• Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
October 17, 2017 
Anthony H. Trembley 
6461 San Onofre Drive 
Camarillo, CA 93012 
Re: 
Your Request for Informal Assistance 
Our File No. 1-17-217 
Dear Mr. Trembley: 
This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict of interest provisions of 
the Political Reform Act (the "Act").1 Because your question is general in nature, we are treating 
your request as one for informal assistance.2 
Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the 
Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of 
interest or Section I 090. 
Also note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 
FPPC Ops. 71 ), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is 
not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for 
additional advice. 
QUESTIONS 
I. Following your withdrawal from the law firm in which you do not hold an ownership 
interest, what financial interests will you continue to have for purposes of the conflict of interest 
provisions of the Act? 
2. How do you comply with the "reason to know" standard in determining whether a 
decision will affect a source of income? 
CONCLUSIONS 
1. Following your withdrawal, you have an interest in the law firm as a source of income 
that disqualifies you from taking part in a decision with a reasonably foreseeable material effect on 
1 The Political Refonn Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 
 lnfonnal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or fonnal 
written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3).) 


File No. 1-17-217 
Page No. 2 
the finn. Your interest in the finn is potentially disqualifying for 12 months following your final 
receipt of income from the Finn. 
2. As a general rule, an official "has reason to know" that a decision will affect a source of 
income whenever a reasonable person, under the same circumstances, would be likely to know or 
should know the identity of the source of income and should be aware or is aware of the decision's 
probable impact on the source. The public official must consider all the infonnation available, 
including any special knowledge he or she may have about a situation, and he or she must follow­
up on available facts which give notice that further inquiry is warranted. 
FACTS 
You assumed office as an elected Council Member for the City of Camarillo on 
December 7, 2016. You are also an attorney in private practice. You retired from full-time practice 
and withdrew from your current law firm (the "Firm") effective September 30, 2017. You were not 
an equity partner in the Finn. In a telephone conversation on October 11, 2017, you noted that you 
did receive annual bonusses based on your overall performance. 
You state that clients of the Finn periodically have business with the City. These clients 
have paid fees to your firm. During your term on the Council, you recused yourself from matters 
involving Firm clients, including any items on the Council's agenda. Before Council decisions, you 
have previously requested that the Firm run a conflict of interest check to detennine whether 
matters on the City Council agenda involved a client of the Firm. Since your withdrawal, you are no 
longer affiliated with the Firm and thus not able to make or receive those checks. You also no 
longer receive income from the Firm. 
ANALYSIS 
Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or using 
his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial 
interest. Section 87103 provides that a public official has a "financial interest" in a governmental 
decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect on one 
or more of the public official's interests. Section 87103 also sets forth the interests from which a 
conflict of interest may arise under the Act. The interests that can give rise to conflicts of interest 
under the Act and that are pertinent to your request are the following: 
• Business Entity- A public official has an interest in a business entity in which he or she has 
a direct or indirect investment of$2,000 or more (Section 87103(a)); or in which he or she is 
a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds any position of management (Section 
87103(d).) 
• Sources of Income - A public official has an interest in any source of income, including 
promised income, aggregating $500 or more within 12 months prior to the decision (Section 
87103(c).) 


File No. 1-17-217 
Page No. 3 
In addition, income of an individual includes a pro rata share of the income of a business 
entity or trust in which the individual or spouse owns, directly, indirectly, or beneficially, a 10 
percent interest or greater. (Section 82030(a).) 
You note that you are not an equity partner in the Finn. As you are no longer an employee 
of the Finn, you no longer have an interest in the Finn as a business entity. Further, since you do 
not have an ownership interest in the law firm, the "l 0-percent rule" does not apply, and you do not 
have an interest in clients of the firm you did not personally represent.3 Based upon the facts 
provided, the Finn is a source of income, and you have an interest in the Finn for 12 months 
following your final receipt of income from the Finn. You would have a conflict of interest in a 
decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect on the 
Frim itself.4 
The "Knows or Has Reason to Know" Standard 
Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or 
otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the 
official "knows or has reason to know" he or she has a financial interest. As a general rule, an 
official "has reason to know" that a decision will affect a source of income whenever a reasonable 
person, under the same circumstances, would be likely to know the identity of the source of income 
and would be aware of the decision's probable impact on the source. (Price Advice Letter, No. A-
85-165; Ewing Advice Letter, No. 1-03-291 ). Whether a public official knows or has reason to 
know is necessarily a factual question and must be determined on a case-by-case basis. 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
By: 
ZWN:jgl 
Sincerely, 
Jack Woodside 
General Counsel 
lmym 
Senior Counsel, Legal Division 
3 While we have generally advised that a non-equity associate has no interest in the clients of the firm, in 
certain circumstances we have found an interest in a client. For example, if a client had authority over the associate• s 
continued employment, we would pierce through the employer and find the client was a source of income. (See, Stone 
Advice Letter, No. A-16-013 citing to the Dorsey Advice Letter, No. A-87-176.) To the extent that a client for whom 
you have performed a significant amount work in the recent past is implicated by a decision, you should seek further 
advice. 
4 If you need assistance determining whether a decision may have a reasonably foreseeable financial effect on 
the Firm, including any decision in which the Firm is representing a person before the City, you should seek further 
advice.
